text: The robotic system is used to perform an iliopubic tract repair on an indirect inguinal hernia, with precise manipulation of instruments visible within the surgical field.
There has always been a need to perform inguinal hernia repair without mesh.
These repairs are typically performed in open anterior fashion.
The posterior approach, while not common, is also described.
The first description was by Cheadle in
This approach was refined and best described in detail as an established technique by Dr. Lloyd Nihus in
The surgeon performs the Nihus repair by suturing the transversalis arch to the iliopubic tract, visibly closing the inguinal defects.
This approach was first described laparoscopically by Gazi-Yerli in
However, it was noted that the suturing required was very technically challenging using traditional laparoscopic instruments.
Using robotics, this challenge may be surmounted and we have employed this technique to repair inguinal hernias in a variety of settings.
The patient is positioned supine with arms tucked, and a Foley catheter is inserted in preparation for the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon gains access using a varus needle and places three 8mm robotic ports for the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates a right-sided inguinal hernia repair using robotic assistance, with the surgical field visible through the robotic ports.
The surgeon identifies the round ligament and medial umbilical ligaments, visually distinguishing these anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The internal ring with an indirect defect is visible, along with the right inferior epigastric artery, during the robotic-assisted hernia repair.
The surgeon continues the dissection and repair of the hernia using the standard robotic Transabdominal Preperitoneal (TAPP) approach, with robotic instruments visible manipulating the tissue.
The surgeon takes down a peritoneal flap to access the preperitoneal space, revealing underlying anatomical structures for the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies the iliopubic tract lateral to the right inferior epigastric artery during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, clearly visualizing the internal ring and indirect defect within the surgical field.
The surgeon inspects the medial space for the presence of a direct or femoral hernia component, examining the area for any visible defects.
While a direct component may be repaired using the robotic ileopubic tract repair, patients with a femoral component must have their hernia addressed via a different technique.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the identified hernia defects, clearly visualizing key anatomical structures in preparation for repair.
In female patients, the surgeon sacrifices the round ligament, which is visually identified and dissected during the hernia repair procedure.

The surgeon identifies the branches of the genitofemoral nerve and the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve as landmarks during the repair of an indirect hernia defect, ensuring these nerves are not entrapped in the process.
The nerve becomes visible in the surgical field as the dissection progresses, allowing for careful identification and avoidance during the hernia repair.
The genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve is visible, passing just inferior to the iliopubic tract.
The surgeon identifies the femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve laterally, ensuring it is visible and not entrapped during the hernia repair procedure.
The femoral branch is observed diving inferior to the iliopubic tract, visible as it passes beneath this anatomical structure during the hernia repair procedure.

The surgeon identifies the typical course of the genitofemoral nerve branches and the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, distinguishing them from nearby vessels and other tubular structures in the surgical field.

The surgeon completes the hernia repair by approximating the transversalis arch to the iliopubic tract, positioned between the internal ring and the femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve, with the nerves clearly identified.
The surgeon performs suturing in an inferior to superior direction, securing the transversalis arch to the iliopubic tract as part of the hernia repair procedure.

The surgeon carefully places sutures to incorporate only the transversus abdominis muscle, avoiding full thickness suturing that could include the internal oblique and risk entrapment of the ilioinguinal nerve.
The surgeon performs the hernia repair using a running barbed suture, visibly securing the transversalis arch to the iliopubic tract.
However, the same results can be achieved with interrupted sutures, as was originally described by Nihus.
For a direct hernia repair, the surgeon closes the defect by suturing the iliopubic tract to the transversalis arch medially, following a similar technique as previously described.
The video displays the completed repair of both direct and indirect hernia defects, with sutures visibly securing the iliopubic tract to the transversalis arch.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum using a running barbed absorbable suture, following the repair of the hernia defect.
This concludes our video description of the robotic iliopubic tract repair.
